retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Myocardial Infarction -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Gross alterations become evident after 6 to 12 hours. Gross pathology reveals evidence of transmural infarction or myocardial necrosis in most cases of occlusion myocardial infarction (OMI). However, within hours of infarction, histo-chemically, the necrotic area appears pale with triphenyl tetrazolium chloride, indicating a lack of staining, whereas the uninfarcted area appears brick red. [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Myocardial Infarction -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The gross pathological, histochemical, light microscopic, and ultrastructural findings vary with coronary occlusion and ischemia onset.

retrieved_docs.title: Silent Myocardial Ischemia -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Histological findings become evident if the ischemic state remains long enough to initiate myocardial damage. The following findings may be appreciated in the setting of acute myocardial infarction.

retrieved_docs.title: Anterior Myocardial Infarction -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: The primary sequence of events in myocardial infarction involves hemorrhagic myocardial necrosis, followed by contraction band necrosis, and eventually, delayed inflammation and repair.

retrieved_docs.title: Posterior Myocardial Ischemia (Archived) -- Evaluation -- Biomarkers
retrieved_docs.content: Troponin elevation greater than the 99th percentile of the upper limit of normal for the particular assay plus one of the following is criteria for acute MI: Symptoms of myocardial ischemia New ischemic ECG changes Development of pathological Q waves Imaging evidence of new loss of viable myocardium or new regional wall motion abnormalities in a pattern consistent with an ischemia etiology Identification of a coronary thrombus by angiography or autopsy [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Infarction -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: The three components in the evaluation of the MI are clinical features, ECG findings, and cardiac biomarkers.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI) -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Coronary artery occlusion in animal models shows a "wave-front" of myocardial injury that spreads from the subendocardial myocardium to the subepicardial myocardium, resulting in a transmural infarction that produces ST-segment elevation on surface ECG. [12] Myocardial damage occurs when the blood flow is interrupted, warranting immediate management. Sudden-onset acute ischemia can result in severe microvascular dysfunction.

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Myocardial Infarction -- Histopathology
retrieved_docs.content: Subsequently, nuclei reduction occurs between 1 to 3 days, and then, within the next 3 to 7 days, macrophages emerge to clear apoptotic cells. Granulation tissue appears at 7 to 10 days, and collagen type I deposition occurs after 10 days. Ultimately, after 2 months, the myocardium undergoes scarring.

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Infarction Serum Markers -- Results, Reporting, and Critical Findings
retrieved_docs.content: The results, as mentioned above, vary according to the type of immunoassays as well as quantification methods.

retrieved_docs.title: Myocardial Infarction Serum Markers -- Testing Procedures -- Troponin
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac troponin (cTn) assays, which are widely available, differ substantially from each other. Almost all of these are enzyme-linked immunosorbent assays in which there is a capture antibody that captures the material as well as a tag antibody that labels it. Most assays have monoclonal antibodies as capture antibodies, that are specific for the cTn being measured, either cardiac troponin I (cTnI) or cardiac troponin T (cTnT). In order to increase the amount of protein captured and labeled, more than two antibodies are used very often. Each assay is different depending on the antibodies that are being used. Moreover, detection methods and calibration also vary. Therefore, one cannot substitute the value of the test from one assay for another. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: TIMI Grade Flow -- Clinical Significance -- Definitions of TMP Grades
retrieved_docs.content: TMP Grade 1: Dye slowly enters but fails to exit the microvasculature. There is the ground-glass appearance (“blush”) or opacification of the myocardium in the distribution of the culprit lesion that fails to clear from the microvasculature, and dye staining is present on the next injection (approximately 30 seconds between injections).

retrieved_docs.title: Acute Myocardial Infarction -- Evaluation -- Cardiac Imaging
retrieved_docs.content: Cardiac angiography is used to perform the percutaneous coronary intervention (PCI) procedure or determine obstructions in the coronary vessels. An echocardiogram is used to assess wall motion, degree of valve abnormality, ischemic mitral regurgitation, and presence of cardiac tamponade.

retrieved_docs.title: Lateral Wall Myocardial Infarction -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Patients suspected of having acute LMI should be promptly evaluated with an ECG and a serial cardiac troponins measurement. [7] [8] Recognizing distinct ST-T involvement patterns can aid in the early diagnosis of myocardial infarction.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 2399
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction, in its many clinical presentations—including ST‑segment elevation infarctions caused by occlusion of the left anterior descending artery, its septal or proximal branches, the intermediate artery, the mid‑portion of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending or posterior lateral branches of the right coronary artery, as well as non‑ST‑segment elevation infarctions, widespread infarctions, and those occurring during procedures or postoperatively—always involves injury to the myocardium. Regardless of the specific coronary vessel or segment that is blocked, the pathological finding site for each of these myocardial infarction subtypes is the myocardial tissue itself, underscoring that the heart muscle is the central organ affected across the full spectrum of acute infarct presentations. This unified understanding emphasizes that diagnostic evaluation, therapeutic interventions, and patient monitoring should focus on the extent and location of myocardial damage, irrespective of the particular coronary artery involved or the clinical context of the infarction.

Despite these variations in presentation and vascular involvement, the pathological finding site for all of them is the myocardial tissue, underscoring that myocardial injury is the central feature of acute coronary syndromes regardless of the precise ECG morphology or culprit vessel. Recognizing the specific type of infarction and its associated arterial occlusion aids clinicians in tailoring reperfusion strategies and optimizing patient care while acknowledging that the underlying myocardial damage remains the pivotal concern.

Morphologically, acute infarct tissue links postoperative acute myocardial infarction and anterior non‑ST‑segment‑elevation myocardial infarction, indicating shared pathological changes across these clinical presentations. Acute subendocardial infarction is classified under “other specified forms of acute ischemic heart disease,” connecting it to a broader group of acute ischemic cardiac conditions. Together, these relationships underscore that coronary artery obstruction at diverse arterial segments is the primary mechanism driving myocardial damage, and that the common histopathology and diagnostic classification of the various AMI phenotypes are essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and targeted patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 1103
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction encompasses a spectrum of disorders that all involve injury to the myocardium. Specific forms include Acute Q‑wave infarction – widespread, Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI), and Acute STEMI due to occlusion of distinct coronary branches such as the left coronary artery, the left anterior descending (LAD) artery, the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the diagonal branch of the LAD, the intermediate artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, and the septal branch of the LAD. Additional variants are Acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to circumflex branch occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction occurring during a procedure, and Acute widespread myocardial infarction. Despite the differing anatomic sites of arterial blockage and electrocardiographic patterns (Q‑wave versus ST‑segment elevation), each of these entities shares the common finding site of the myocardium, underscoring that the primary pathological substrate in all these conditions is myocardial tissue damage. Recognizing the specific coronary artery involved helps guide reperfusion strategies and risk stratification, while the universal involvement of the myocardium highlights the need for prompt diagnosis and treatment to preserve cardiac function.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 1153
retrieved_docs.content: Acute myocardial infarction (AMI) is a spectrum of coronary syndromes in which the primary site of injury is the myocardium, manifesting with a characteristic “acute infarct” morphology that includes early necrosis, inflammation and edema of cardiac myocytes. The spectrum encompasses acute Q‑wave infarction (transmural necrosis with a specific Q‑wave morphology), acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) of various electrocardiographic patterns, acute non‑Q‑wave infarction, acute non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (NSTEMI), acute widespread myocardial infarction, postoperative acute myocardial infarction, acute myocardial infarction occurring during a procedure, and subsequent anterior non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction. All of these clinical entities are linked to occlusion of specific coronary arterial branches, each producing a localized myocardial injury that corresponds to the territory supplied by the blocked vessel. Reported occlusion sites include the proximal, mid and distal segments of the left anterior descending (LAD) artery, the left coronary artery (LCA) as a whole, the circumflex branch of the LCA, the diagonal and septal branches of the LAD, the intermediate artery, and numerous branches of the right coronary artery (RCA) such as its proximal, mid, distal, marginal, posterior descending, posterior‑lateral and septal branches. Occlusion of any of these vessels generates the hallmark ST‑segment elevation on the electrocardiogram (in STEMI) while the underlying pathology remains confined to myocardial tissue. Recognizing the precise relationship between the anatomic location of coronary blockage, the resulting electrocardiographic pattern and the uniform acute infarct histopathology is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification and timely reperfusion therapy in patients with acute coronary syndromes.

Collectively, the interconnections among acute myocardial infarction, various forms of heart muscle injury, coronary arterial thrombotic events, associated arrhythmias, and related systemic conditions illustrate the need for integrated diagnostic and therapeutic strategies in cardiovascular medicine.

This common involvement of the myocardial tissue underscores that, regardless of the specific electrocardiographic pattern (Q‑wave, non‑Q‑wave, ST‑segment elevation, or non‑ST‑segment elevation) or the particular coronary artery segment that is occluded (including the left anterior descending, intermediate, right coronary, posterior‑lateral, septal, or circumflex branches), the primary pathological substrate is injury to the heart muscle itself. Recognizing the myocardium as the shared site of injury across these diverse presentations is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and the implementation of targeted therapeutic strategies aimed at preserving myocardial function and improving patient outcomes.
